headline: Chip Stocks Crater as Hormuz Fears Lift Energy; Market Breadth Holds Positive Despite Semiconductor Rout
body_html:
Monday's mid-session picture is one of sharp internal divergence. Semiconductors have emerged as the session's clear casualty, sliding 3.61% as a group — by far the worst-performing sector in the universe — while geopolitical turbulence in the Middle East rattles supply-chain and demand assumptions for the technology complex. Sandisk leads all decliners, off nearly 9%, with Arm Holdings shedding close to 8% and Marvell Technology, Seagate, and Western Digital each posting losses exceeding 6%. Rambus rounds out a deeply bruised chip cohort.

The damage in semiconductors has not, however, dragged the broader tape into the red. Of 395 names in the universe, 239 are trading higher against 150 lower — a respectable advance-decline ratio that reflects genuine strength elsewhere. Energy is the session's standout sector, adding 1.16%, as Iran's widened strikes in the Gulf and a sharp slowdown in Hormuz shipping traffic push oil higher. Texas Pacific Land has surged nearly 4%, Valero Energy is up 3.35%, and Core Laboratories has added close to 3%, illustrating how royalty, refining, and services names alike are catching a bid.

Consumer names are also performing well, with Deckers Brands up 3.5% and H&R Block gaining 3.33%, helping lift the consumer sector 0.94% on the day. Software, Materials, Healthcare, Homebuilders, and Industrials are all modestly positive. Into the close, watch whether escalating Hormuz rhetoric triggers a further rotation out of tech and into defensive energy plays, and whether the chip selloff deepens or finds stabilization in the final hour.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Shipping Slows to Two-Month Low Amid Renewed Strikes
briefs.body: Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen to its lowest pace in two months as fresh exchanges of fire between U.S. and Iranian forces raise safety concerns for commercial vessels. The slowdown is amplifying supply anxieties in already tight energy markets, providing direct fuel for Monday's rally in crude-linked equities.

briefs.headline: Iran Expands Strikes on U.S. Positions Across the Gulf Region
briefs.body: Iranian forces have broadened their targeting of American military installations throughout the Gulf, according to reports, elevating geopolitical risk across regional trade routes. The escalation has pushed oil prices sharply higher, rattled Indian equity markets, and is exerting fresh pressure on Asian currencies with heavy crude-import exposures, including the Indian rupee.

briefs.headline: Trump Floats U.S. Toll for Hormuz Passage, Rattling Diplomatic Waters
briefs.body: President Trump suggested Monday that the United States should receive financial compensation for its role in securing the Strait of Hormuz and asserted that Washington should exercise control over the critical waterway. The comments add a new diplomatic dimension to an already volatile standoff and have amplified uncertainty among shipping and energy traders.

briefs.headline: Europe's Jet Fuel Stockpiles Dangerously Thin as Middle East Crisis Flares
briefs.body: European jet fuel inventories stand at less than one month's supply, a precarious level that coincides with the renewed flare-up in Hormuz tensions. Analysts warn that any sustained disruption to Gulf crude flows could rapidly translate into fuel shortages and sharply higher operating costs for European carriers heading into peak summer travel season.

briefs.headline: Houthis Threaten Retaliation After Accusing Saudi Arabia of Sanaa Strike
briefs.body: Houthi forces accused Saudi Arabia of striking Sanaa's airport Monday and pledged to respond with force, adding another flashpoint to an already combustible regional situation. The threat widens the theatre of potential conflict beyond U.S.-Iran exchanges and introduces additional tail risk for Gulf infrastructure and regional energy logistics.

briefs.headline: Gold Retreats as Tension-Driven Rate Fears Overshadow Safe-Haven Demand
briefs.body: Gold prices extended their decline Monday despite the surge in geopolitical anxiety, as markets recalibrated expectations toward a prolonged higher-interest-rate environment. The dynamic suggests that inflation concerns stoked by rising oil prices are, for now, outweighing the traditional safe-haven premium that Middle East escalations typically bestow on the precious metal.
